content.claim: Rising socioeconomic and ethnic residential segregation in Finnish cities (with over one-third of Finnish school children in urban areas) transmits into schools through residential-assignment and school-choice mechanisms, placing municipalities in a structural tension between market-enrolment rules and equality imperatives.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Finnish system reform lens
limitations: Describes structural dynamic but does not quantify segregation magnitude or isolate school-choice contribution relative to residential factors
'Market imperative' is a conceptual framing; specific policy mechanisms by which municipalities manage this tension are not detailed
Findings are Nordic/OECD-level observation not specific to quantified Hungarian context
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
